serving.natural_language_portion: One nabe sea bass set meal typically comprises approximately 550 g total, including a portion of simmered sea bass with vegetables in dashi broth, a bowl of steamed white rice (~150 g), and a small miso soup.

nutrient_source.title: Sea bass (raw, cooked) and Japanese hot pot composite — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Fish, sea bass, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175159); Japanese white rice cooked (FDC ID 169756); Tofu, firm (FDC ID 172476); Vegetables (cabbage, mushrooms) standard entries; Miso soup estimated from USDA Branded/SR Legacy components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a nabe sea bass set meal as a composite dish. Nutrient values per 100 g are estimated by weighted component averaging: sea bass cooked (~30% by weight), steamed white rice (~27%), tofu (~10%), mixed vegetables including napa cabbage, shiitake, enoki, spring onion (~20%), dashi-soy broth (~13%). USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries used for all components. Values represent a plausible central estimate for this restaurant-style set meal.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; white rice cooked GI ~72 used as anchor; mixed meal GI estimated at ~48 accounting for protein, fat, and fibre attenuation from fish, tofu, and vegetables in hot pot context
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for nabe sea bass set meal. White rice cooked has a GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database). However, this is a mixed set meal where sea bass protein (~8.5 g/100 g), fat (~1.8 g/100 g), dietary fibre (~0.8 g/100 g), and tofu contribute to significant glycaemic attenuation. Mixed meal GI estimated at 48 (low category) based on established mixed-meal GI reduction principles documented in Atkinson et al. 2021 (Diabetes Care) and Jenkins et al. peer-reviewed literature on protein-fat-fibre attenuation of rice-based meals. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (48 × 66.0) / 100 = 31.7, where carbohydrate per 550 g serving ≈ 66 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from dashi broth and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: The dashi-soy broth base contributes approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g (estimated ~1705 mg per set meal), which represents a significant proportion of the 2000 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-sodium broth or consume only a portion of the broth.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycaemic load from white rice component, attenuated by fish protein and vegetables
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycaemic load of approximately 31.7 per set meal is moderate; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider requesting a smaller rice portion or substituting with brown rice to reduce postprandial glucose excursion, while the sea bass and tofu protein content supports satiety and glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean fish protein with omega-3 fatty acids and low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sea bass provides lean protein and omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (~0.6 g/100 g) with low saturated fat (~0.4 g/100 g), which is broadly cardioprotective; however, the sodium content from the broth warrants monitoring in patients with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nabe Sea Bass Set (鍋スズキセット) is a Japanese hot pot set meal featuring sea bass (suzuki) simmered in a light dashi-based broth seasoned with soy sauce and mirin, accompanied by tofu, napa cabbage, shiitake and enoki mushrooms, and spring onion. The set is served as a complete meal with a bowl of steamed white rice and a small miso soup. The dish originates from the Japanese nabemono (hot pot) tradition, where ingredients are gently simmered at the table or in the kitchen in a clay or iron pot, producing a delicate, umami-rich broth. Sea bass (Lateolabrax japonicus or Dicentrarchus labrax) is a lean, mild white fish with a firm yet tender texture that absorbs the dashi broth flavours well. The overall flavour profile is savoury, light, and clean, with subtle sweetness from mirin and depth from kombu and bonito dashi. Nutritionally, the set meal provides approximately 98 kcal per 100 g, with a meaningful protein contribution (~8.5 g/100 g) from sea bass and tofu, low saturated fat (~0.4 g/100 g), and moderate carbohydrate from the rice component (~12 g/100 g). The estimated glycaemic index of the mixed meal is approximately 48 (low category), attenuated by the protein, fat, and fibre from fish, tofu, and vegetables. Sodium content is moderate-to-high due to the soy-dashi broth (~310 mg/100 g), which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free and suitable for pescatarians. It is commonly served in Japanese seafood restaurants in Singapore, including GyoGyo at Funan mall.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite restaurant set meal. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component averaging of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for sea bass (cooked), white rice (cooked), tofu, and mixed vegetables. GI is an analogous estimate based on mixed-meal attenuation principles from peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium rather than low because all component sources are approved primary sources and the estimation methodology is documented and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries using weighted averaging across the set meal composition (sea bass ~30%, rice ~27%, tofu ~10%, vegetables ~20%, broth ~13% by weight). 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — sea bass and plant-based components contain negligible industrially produced trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component entries). 3. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for nabe sea bass set meal exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Sodium estimate (~310 mg/100 g) is sensitive to broth volume consumed; actual intake may vary significantly depending on how much broth is consumed. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component entries and represent plausible central estimates rather than analytically measured values for this specific dish. 6. The restaurant identifier '402D' in the original food name appears to be a menu item code used by GyoGyo - Funan; it is preserved in aliases but not used as the canonical food name. 7. Halal status is not confirmed; GyoGyo uses Japanese ingredients including mirin (rice wine) which may not be halal-certified; is_halal set to false pending confirmation.
